---
title: Nordic nRF53 series
description: Nordic Semiconductor's nRF5340 — dual Cortex-M33 with TrustZone, 128 MHz application core, 64 MHz network core running BLE/Thread/Matter. Inter-processor communication, nrfx HAL, Zephyr-based nRF Connect SDK.
order: 15
updated: 2026-05-13
tags: [vendors, nordic, nrf53, nrf5340, cortex-m33, ble, thread, matter, zephyr]
---

Nordic Semiconductor built its market position on the nRF51 (Cortex-M0) and nRF52 (Cortex-M4F) lines — single-core wireless SoCs that became the default choice for BLE wearables, beacons, and sensor nodes. The nRF53 series is the architectural reset: two Cortex-M33 cores on one die, ARM TrustZone, and a hard split between the application world and the radio world. Where the nRF52 ran the BLE stack alongside the application on a single M4F (with strict priority discipline), the nRF53 puts them on physically separate cores that communicate through hardware mailboxes.

This is the same dual-M33 pattern that NXP LPC55S69 and STM32WBA hint at but never quite committed to — Nordic has gone all-in. The result is a part that can run Matter, Thread border routing, and BLE Mesh simultaneously without ever causing the radio's hard real-time constraints to leak into the application core.

<SpecSheet

    { label: 'TrustZone', value: 'Full ARMv8-M; SPU (System Protection Unit) partitions peripherals between secure/non-secure' },

    { label: 'Crypto', value: 'CryptoCell-312: AES-128/256, ECC P-256, SHA-256, ChaCha20-Poly1305, TRNG NIST SP 800-90B' },
    { label: 'Process', value: '22 nm ULP — significantly newer than nRF52 (40 nm)' },
    { label: 'Power', value: 'System OFF: 1.4 µA; System ON RAM-retained: 2.8 µA; CPU active radio off: ~5 mA @ 128 MHz' },
  ]}
/>

## Why two cores

A BLE link layer has microsecond-precision real-time requirements. The radio's "anchor point" — the start of a connection event — is scheduled by hardware, and the link-layer ISR must service that anchor within ~150 µs or the connection drops a packet. On a single-core nRF52, the link-layer ISR runs at the highest priority and the application must yield to it. This works but constrains the application: long interrupt-disabled regions break the link, deep callback chains can blow the stack-budget headroom, and any code path that delays an ISR by more than 150 µs is a bug.

The nRF53 makes this no longer a shared-core problem. The application core runs the application; the network core runs the radio. Each has its own ISR vector, its own scheduling, its own NVIC. They share Flash (partitioned) and a small amount of RAM (for IPC mailboxes), but they execute independently. An application bug that holds the application core in a critical section for 50 ms does not affect the BLE link.

/>

The application core owns most peripherals: GPIO, SPI, I2C, UART, USB, PWM, ADC, the timers, the crypto block. The network core owns the radio, the radio timers, and a slim set of peripherals it needs to support time-deterministic radio operation. The SPU (System Protection Unit) is the gatekeeper — at boot time the application core programs it to assign each peripheral to either secure or non-secure execution, and the SPU enforces those assignments.

## The IPC mechanism

The IPC peripheral has 16 channels, each with two halves: a sender side and a receiver side. Posting to a channel costs one register write; the receiver wakes via interrupt or polls the channel-active register.

```c
// Application core — send a message
shared_buffer.cmd = CMD_START_ADVERTISING;
shared_buffer.params = &adv_params;
NRF_IPC->TASKS_SEND[CHANNEL_TX] = 1;          // post to channel
// Network core's IPC ISR fires immediately
```

In practice, this raw IPC layer is wrapped by HCI (Host Controller Interface for BLE) — the standard Bluetooth-SIG protocol that lets a host talk to a BLE controller. The application core runs the BLE host (GAP, GATT, security manager); the network core runs the BLE controller (link layer, link manager, PHY). HCI commands flow application → network; HCI events flow back. Same protocol you'd use over UART HCI to a discrete BLE chip, but the transport is the on-chip IPC.

For Thread, Zigbee, and Matter, the split is similar — the network core runs the 802.15.4 MAC and the IPv6 6LoWPAN layer, the application core runs the higher protocol (CoAP for Matter, ZCL for Zigbee).

## TrustZone partitioning

The nRF53's TrustZone is fully implemented. The application core boots into secure state. Application firmware splits into:

- **Secure firmware** — typically TF-M (Trusted Firmware-M), a reference implementation of PSA Functional API services. Manages crypto keys, secure boot, secure storage, attestation.
- **Non-secure firmware** — the customer's application code. Calls into TF-M for any crypto or secure-storage operation.

The SPU partitions Flash regions (8 KB granularity), RAM regions (also 8 KB), and individual peripheral instances. Once configured, the SPU locks until reset — non-secure code cannot re-allocate itself secure access.

This is the architectural pattern recommended by Arm's PSA Certified scheme. nRF53 was one of the first MCUs to achieve PSA Certified Level 2, and Nordic's Zephyr-based SDK ships TF-M as a build-time option for the secure side.

<Callout type="tip">
For a first-time nRF53 project, start with the SDK's "ble peripheral hr" sample built with `CONFIG_BUILD_WITH_TFM=y`. This gives you a working secure/non-secure split, TF-M running, BLE heart-rate service on non-secure, all the SPU partitioning correctly configured. From there you replace the non-secure application with your own; the TF-M side rarely needs changes.
</Callout>

## nRF Connect SDK — Zephyr-based

Nordic's nRF Connect SDK is built on Zephyr. The application core runs Zephyr; the network core runs a Nordic-supplied closed binary (for BLE/Thread/Matter) or, increasingly, an open-source Zephyr image with the OpenThread / Bluetooth controller stacks. The shift to open-source network-core images is recent (2023+) and means you can debug and modify the radio stack source rather than treat it as a black box.

The SDK build flow is multi-image:

```
$ west build -b nrf5340dk_nrf5340_cpuapp samples/bluetooth/peripheral_hr
```

This builds the application core image (`build/zephyr/zephyr.hex`) and, automatically, the network core image (`build/hci_ipc/zephyr/zephyr.hex`). Both get flashed; the application core boots into its image, the network core boots into its image, IPC connects them.

Compared to the nRF52 Soft Device model (where the BLE stack is a precompiled binary you flash separately and call via SVC), the nRF53 model is more flexible — different network-core images give you different protocols, and you can mix-and-match at build time.

| Feature | nRF52 (single core) | nRF53 (dual core) |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Cores | 1× M4F @ 64 MHz | M33 @ 128 + M33 @ 64 |
| Radio stack | "Soft Device" binary | Network-core image |
| Stack call mechanism | SVC instruction | IPC mailbox |
| Application can starve radio? | Yes (priority bug) | Architecturally no |
| TrustZone | No | Yes |
| FPU | Yes | Yes (app core only) |
| Process node | 40 nm | 22 nm |
| Power (CPU active) | ~3 mA @ 64 MHz | ~3.5 mA @ 128 MHz (app), ~2.5 mA radio off |
| Cost (1k qty) | ~$2.50 | ~$5 |

## nRF91 sibling

The nRF91 series (nRF9160, nRF9151) is similar in spirit — single Cortex-M33 with TrustZone, but pairs it with an integrated LTE-M / NB-IoT / GPS modem instead of a 2.4 GHz radio. Same Zephyr-based SDK, same TF-M secure model. Covered separately under [Nordic nRF91](/docs/vendors/nordic-nrf91).

## Power and certification

The 22 nm process is the unsung win of nRF53. Sleep currents are notably better than nRF52 — System OFF retention at 1.4 µA, plus selectively powered RAM at hundreds of nA per 8 KB block. For coin-cell wearables and sensor tags, this directly translates into months more runtime versus a same-mAh nRF52 design.

Pre-certified hardware modules from Nordic and third parties (Fanstel, Insight SiP, Raytac) wrap the nRF5340 die in an FCC/CE-certified shielded package. Designing with a module shortcuts certification (you inherit the module's intentional-radiator certification and only need unintentional-radiator testing on the rest of the product). For low-volume products this is the right starting point; for high volume you go bare-die and run your own certification.

<Callout type="warn">
The nRF5340 datasheet's "5 mA @ 128 MHz" CPU current is at minimum-feature operating conditions. Real applications running TLS handshakes plus crypto engagement plus radio events pull substantially more average current. Always budget power from a measured prototype, not from datasheet best-case.
</Callout>
